lessons.title: CRM Architecture: Contacts, Deals, and the Pipeline View
lessons.title_es: Arquitectura del CRM: Contactos, Tratos y la Vista de Pipeline
lessons.description: Understand how NetWeb CRM structures data across contacts, deals, and pipeline stages to give your team a single source of truth.
lessons.description_es: Comprende cómo NetWeb CRM estructura datos entre contactos, tratos y etapas de pipeline para dar a tu equipo una fuente única de verdad.
lessons.content:
The Core Data Model

NetWeb CRM is built on three primary objects: Contacts, Deals, and Organizations. Every interaction — email, call, form submission, chat — attaches to one of these objects. Understanding their relationship is the foundation of every workflow you'll build.

Contacts represent individual people. They carry lifecycle stage (Lead → MQL → SQL → Customer → Churned), source attribution, tags, and a full activity timeline. A contact can belong to multiple organizations and be associated with multiple deals simultaneously — this matters for B2B accounts where you have multiple stakeholders per deal.

Deals live in a pipeline with configurable stages. Each deal has a monetary value, expected close date, probability, and an owner. The pipeline view calculates weighted revenue automatically: value × probability = weighted forecast.

Setting Up Your Pipeline in 5 Steps

  • Step 1 — Define stages: Go to Settings → Pipeline → Add Stage. Start with: New Lead, Contacted, Qualified, Proposal Sent, Negotiation, Closed Won, Closed Lost.
  • Step 2 — Set stage probabilities: Assign a default close probability to each stage (New Lead = 10%, Qualified = 40%, Proposal Sent = 65%, Negotiation = 80%). This powers your forecast.
  • Step 3 — Create required fields per stage: Use stage entry requirements to force reps to capture budget, timeline, and decision-maker before advancing a deal. This kills forecast contamination.
  • Step 4 — Set rotting rules: Enable deal rotting alerts — any deal without activity for X days turns red. Recommended: 7 days for high-velocity, 21 days for enterprise.
  • Step 5 — Connect your forms: Under Integrations → Forms, map each form submission to a pipeline stage. Audit form → New Lead. Demo request → Qualified.

The Kanban view is best for individual reps managing their own book. The List view with custom columns is better for managers reviewing the whole team's pipeline in bulk. Switch between them using the toggle in the top-right of the pipeline screen.

A common mistake: creating too many pipeline stages. If a stage doesn't require a different action from your team, it doesn't need to exist. Five to seven stages is optimal for most SMBs.

lessons.title: Marketing Module: Campaigns, Sequences, and Lead Scoring
lessons.title_es: Módulo de Marketing: Campañas, Secuencias y Puntuación de Leads
lessons.description: Build email campaigns and automated nurture sequences, then use lead scoring to surface your hottest prospects automatically.
lessons.description_es: Construye campañas de email y secuencias de nutrición automatizadas, luego usa la puntuación de leads para identificar tus prospectos más calientes automáticamente.
lessons.content:
Campaign vs. Sequence: Know the Difference

NetWeb CRM has two distinct outbound tools: Campaigns (broadcast, one-time sends to a list segment) and Sequences (automated, time-delayed drip programs triggered by an event). Using the wrong one is the most common mistake new users make.

Use a Campaign when: you're sending a newsletter, a product announcement, or a promotional offer to a defined segment. Campaigns are one-to-many, sent once, and not personalized beyond merge tags.

Use a Sequence when: a contact performs an action (fills a form, books a demo, goes silent for 30 days) and you want a series of touches to follow. Sequences are trigger-based and respect contact behavior — if they reply, the sequence pauses automatically.

Building a 5-Touch Nurture Sequence

  • Touch 1 (Day 0): Welcome email. Deliver the lead magnet or confirm the action they took. Subject line = what they get, not who you are. Send immediately.
  • Touch 2 (Day 2): Value email. One specific insight, tip, or case study relevant to their industry. No ask. 150 words max.
  • Touch 3 (Day 5): Social proof. One customer story in 3 sentences: situation, action, result. Link to full case study.
  • Touch 4 (Day 9): Objection handling. Address the top 2 reasons people don't buy. Frame as FAQ.
  • Touch 5 (Day 14): Soft CTA. Ask if they want a 15-minute call. Include a Calendly link. Subject:

.

Lead Scoring: The BANT-Behavior Matrix

Lead scoring in NetWeb CRM assigns point values to profile attributes and behavioral signals. Contacts crossing a threshold (typically 50 points) trigger an alert to your sales team.

Recommended scoring model:

  • Opens an email: +2 points
  • Clicks a link: +5 points
  • Visits pricing page: +15 points
  • Requests a demo: +30 points
  • Job title is decision-maker (Owner, VP, Director): +10 points
  • Company size 10–200 employees: +10 points
  • Unsubscribes: −50 points (remove from sequences)

Set up scoring under Contacts → Lead Scoring → Scoring Rules. Connect it to a pipeline automation: when score ≥ 50, create a deal in
stage and notify the deal owner via Slack.

lessons.title: Service Module: Tickets, SLAs, and the Customer Health Score
lessons.title_es: Módulo de Servicio: Tickets, SLAs y la Puntuación de Salud del Cliente
lessons.description: Set up the service desk with SLA rules, ticket routing, and the health score model that predicts churn 60 days in advance.
lessons.description_es: Configura el servicio de atención con reglas de SLA, enrutamiento de tickets y el modelo de salud del cliente que predice el abandono con 60 días de anticipación.
lessons.content:
Ticket Lifecycle and Routing

Every customer-facing issue in NetWeb CRM becomes a Ticket. Tickets have a status (Open, In Progress, Waiting on Customer, Resolved, Closed), a priority (Low, Medium, High, Critical), and an owner. The routing engine assigns tickets automatically based on rules you define — no manual triage required.

Configuring SLA Policies

SLA (Service Level Agreement) policies define how fast your team must respond and resolve tickets based on priority and customer tier. Set them under Service → SLA Policies.

  • Critical: First response ≤ 1 hour, Resolution ≤ 4 hours. Escalate to manager if breached.
  • High: First response ≤ 4 hours, Resolution ≤ 24 hours.
  • Medium: First response ≤ 8 hours, Resolution ≤ 3 business days.
  • Low: First response ≤ 24 hours, Resolution ≤ 5 business days.

When an SLA is at risk (75% of time elapsed), NetWeb CRM sends an automatic notification to the ticket owner and their manager. When it breaches, the ticket turns red and a escalation workflow fires. Configure escalation targets under Service → Escalation Rules.

The Customer Health Score Model

The Health Score is a 0–100 composite metric that rolls up across four dimensions. NetWeb CRM calculates it daily and displays it on every contact and organization record.

  • Usage (30 pts): How frequently is the customer logging in or using your product? Pull data via the API integration or manual check-ins.
  • Engagement (25 pts): Are they opening your emails, attending QBRs, responding to surveys?
  • Support (25 pts): Ticket volume and resolution satisfaction. High open ticket count = score penalty.
  • Financial (20 pts): Payment on time, contract renewal date proximity, upsell potential.

Customers scoring below 40 are At Risk — the system creates an automatic task for their account manager to schedule a check-in call within 5 business days. Below 20 = Critical — escalate to CS director. Customers scoring above 75 are Promoter candidates — trigger a case study request sequence.

Run the health score report monthly under Reporting → Customer Health to spot churn trends before they hit your revenue numbers.

lessons.title: AI Agents in the CRM: Drafting, Summarizing, and Automating Actions
lessons.title_es: Agentes de IA en el CRM: Redacción, Resumen y Automatización de Acciones
lessons.description: Deploy NetWeb CRM's built-in AI layer to draft emails, summarize call transcripts, score deals, and trigger actions without leaving the CRM.
lessons.description_es: Despliega la capa de IA integrada de NetWeb CRM para redactar correos, resumir transcripciones, calificar tratos y disparar acciones sin salir del CRM.
lessons.content:
Three AI Modes in NetWeb CRM

The AI layer in NetWeb CRM operates in three modes: Assist (you initiate, AI drafts), Suggest (AI surfaces recommendations passively), and Automate (AI acts on a trigger without human review). Start with Assist. Promote to Automate only after reviewing 20+ outputs and confirming quality.

Assist Mode: Email Drafting

On any contact or deal record, click the AI button in the activity panel. The AI reads the contact's full timeline — emails sent, tickets opened, deal stage, last interaction date — and drafts a contextually relevant email. The prompt template it uses:

  • Contact name, company, industry
  • Last interaction type and date
  • Current deal stage and value
  • Open tickets (if any)
  • Your specified goal (follow-up, proposal, re-engagement)

Edit the draft before sending. The AI does not send autonomously in Assist mode.

Suggest Mode: Deal Intelligence

In Suggest mode, the AI analyzes your pipeline and surfaces three types of recommendations on the pipeline view:

  • Deal risk flags: Deals that haven't had activity in 14+ days, are missing key fields, or have a close date in the past.
  • Next best action: For each deal, the AI suggests one action (send proposal, schedule call, send case study) based on stage and engagement signals.
  • Forecast variance alerts: When a deal's probability drops significantly (e.g., contact unsubscribed, ticket unresolved), the AI flags it and adjusts the weighted forecast.

Automate Mode: Trigger-Based Actions

Automate mode connects AI to the workflow engine. Example automation:

  • Trigger: Contact visits pricing page 3+ times in 7 days.
  • AI action: Draft and queue a personalized email referencing their company and the plan most relevant to their company size.
  • Human gate: Email goes into an

queue for 2-hour review window, then sends if no human action taken.

Configure Automate workflows under Automation → AI Actions. Always set a human review window of at least 30 minutes for customer-facing outputs. Use the Confidence threshold setting — outputs below 0.75 confidence route to a human queue instead of sending.
